verses.text: Now there cried a certain woman of the wives of the sons of the prophets unto Elisha, saying, Thy servant my husband is dead; and thou knowest that thy servant did fear the LORD: and the creditor is come to take unto him my two sons to be bondmen. Guidance: The widow's cry. The chapter opens with a domestic crisis. A widow — specifically, the widow of one of the 'sons of the prophets' (benei ha-nevi'im) — comes to Elisha with a desperate appeal. Her husband, who had been a member of the prophetic community, is dead. He had borrowed from a creditor during life; the debt is now due; under ancient Israelite law (Lev 25:39; Exod 21:7) a creditor could legally take a debtor's children as indentured servants until the debt was worked off. 'The creditor is come to take unto him my two sons to be bondmen.' Both her sons — her only support and her future — are about to be seized. The widow's appeal contains a small theological argument: 'Thou knowest that thy servant did fear the LORD.' Her late husband had been a fearing-the-LORD man — a prophetic-school member, a man known for his piety. The argument implies: this is a family the LORD's covenant should protect; the indenturing of his sons would dishonor a man who served the LORD faithfully. The chapter is showing us a foundational pattern of Elisha's ministry. Unlike Elijah, whose ministry was primarily public confrontation with kings, Elisha's ministry includes deep care for the obscure and the bereaved. The widow of a prophet-school member has the prophet's full attention. The chapter is teaching us a principle of pastoral leadership: those who minister are owed care, and care for their families when they are gone. The prophetic community has both vertical (toward the LORD) and horizontal (toward each other) obligations. Elisha's role includes both. The chapter is also setting up a contrast with the world we have just left. Chapter 3 was three kings, an army of thousands, an international war. Chapter 4 is one widow and her two sons. The same LORD provides for both situations. The miracle of provision in the wilderness is the same miracle now — at smaller scale, in private setting — for a widow's debt.

And Elisha said unto her, What shall I do for thee? tell me, what hast thou in the house? And she said, Thine handmaid hath not any thing in the house, save a pot of oil. Then he said, Go, borrow thee vessels abroad of all thy neighbours, even empty vessels; borrow not a few. And when thou art come in, thou shalt shut the door upon thee and upon thy sons, and shalt set aside that which is full. Guidance: Elisha's questions and the borrowed vessels. Elisha's response is in the form of two questions. 'What shall I do for thee?' — pastoral solicitation of her actual need; he does not assume he knows. 'Tell me, what hast thou in the house?' — inventory. The miracle will work with what is already available; he is asking her to identify her starting resources. Her answer: 'Thine handmaid hath not any thing in the house, save a pot of oil.' Hebrew kol asukh shamen — 'a flask of oil' — a small ceramic vessel containing perhaps a few cups of olive oil. Almost nothing. But not nothing. The instructions: 'Go, borrow thee vessels abroad of all thy neighbours, even empty vessels; borrow not a few.' Three details. (1) Borrow from neighbors — empty vessels, jars, cooking pots, whatever can hold liquid. (2) 'Borrow not a few' — Hebrew al-tam'iti — 'do not make them few.' Bring many. (3) 'When thou art come in, thou shalt shut the door upon thee and upon thy sons.' The miracle will happen in private — closed doors, with only her and her sons present. 'And shalt set aside that which is full' — as each vessel fills, set it apart. The chapter is teaching us two principles about how the LORD provides through miracles. (1) The miracle uses what is already present, however small. The widow's pot of oil — almost nothing — becomes the seed-quantity that the miracle multiplies. The LORD does not create the oil ex nihilo; He multiplies the oil she has. The chapter is teaching us not to despise the small starting resource. (2) The capacity to receive determines the size of the provision. The widow's harvest will be limited only by the number of vessels she has gathered. The LORD's provision will fill every container she borrows; the moment she runs out of containers, the flow will stop. The chapter is teaching us that the LORD often calibrates His provision to our preparation. Borrow many vessels — receive much oil. Borrow few — receive few. The faith-act precedes the miracle.

So she went from him, and shut the door upon her and upon her sons, who brought the vessels to her; and she poured out. And it came to pass, when the vessels were full, that she said unto her son, Bring me yet a vessel. And he said unto her, There is not a vessel more. And the oil stayed. Then she came and told the man of God. And he said, Go, sell the oil, and pay thy debt, and live thou and thy children of the rest. Guidance: The oil flows; the debt is paid. The widow obeys. She and her sons shut themselves inside; they pour from her one small flask into the borrowed vessel; the sons keep bringing more vessels; the oil keeps flowing. 'When the vessels were full, that she said unto her son, Bring me yet a vessel.' She keeps calling for more containers; the oil keeps flowing. 'And he said unto her, There is not a vessel more. And the oil stayed.' The moment they run out of containers — the oil stops. The narrator notes the exact pacing: oil flows as long as there are vessels; oil ceases when vessels run out. The chapter is showing us that the LORD's provision is plentiful but not random. It flows until preparation runs out. The widow could have borrowed more vessels and received more oil; she could not have received oil without vessels to hold it. She returns to Elisha. His instructions: 'Go, sell the oil, and pay thy debt, and live thou and thy children of the rest.' Three actions: (1) Sell the oil — turn the miracle into liquidity. (2) Pay thy debt — discharge the creditor; rescue the sons. (3) Live thou and thy children of the rest — the surplus becomes the family's sustenance. The chapter is teaching us that the LORD's miracles often have both immediate and ongoing dimensions. The debt-rescue is immediate (the sons are saved from indenture). The 'rest' provides ongoing sustenance. One miracle solves multiple problems. The chapter is also teaching us a stewardship principle: turn the miracle into ordinary provision. The widow does not display the jars of oil as a wonder-cabinet; she sells the oil, settles the debt, and uses the surplus for daily living. The miracle becomes the foundation of ordinary life. The chapter is showing us Elisha's pastoral practicality. Other prophets in other situations might have done dramatic, public miracles. Elisha quietly multiplies oil in a widow's private kitchen so her sons need not be enslaved. The same LORD who stopped Ahab's rain provides the widow's oil. The scale differs; the source is the same.

And it fell on a day, that Elisha passed to Shunem, where was a great woman; and she constrained him to eat bread. And so it was, that as oft as he passed by, he turned in thither to eat bread. And she said unto her husband, Behold now, I perceive that this is an holy man of God, which passeth by us continually. Let us make a little chamber, I pray thee, on the wall; and let us set for him there a bed, and a table, and a stool, and a candlestick: and it shall be, when he cometh to us, that he shall turn in thither. Guidance: The Shunammite woman and the prophet's chamber. The chapter shifts to a new story. Elisha is now traveling through Shunem — a town in the valley of Jezreel, in Issachar's tribal territory, about midway between Mount Carmel (his frequent residence) and Mount Tabor. A 'great woman' (ishah gedolah — a substantial, prosperous woman) of Shunem 'constrained' (vatachazek-bo — 'pressed/seized upon him,' a strong verb) him to eat with her. From then on, whenever Elisha passes through Shunem, he stops at her house for a meal. The hospitality becomes regular. Then she proposes an upgrade. 'Behold now, I perceive that this is an holy man of God.' The Hebrew yodaati ki ish elohim qadosh hu — 'I have known that he is a holy man of God.' Her spiritual perception is accurate; she recognizes Elisha's standing not by demonstration but by his bearing. 'Let us make a little chamber, I pray thee, on the wall; and let us set for him there a bed, and a table, and a stool, and a candlestick.' Four items of furniture: bed, table, stool, candlestick (lampstand). She proposes building him a small permanent room — likely a one-room addition on the flat roof of their house (or on an exterior wall) — fully furnished, available whenever he passes. 'It shall be, when he cometh to us, that he shall turn in thither.' The chapter is showing us hospitality at its most thoughtful. The Shunammite woman does not just feed Elisha; she gives him privacy and rest. The four pieces of furniture address four needs: sleep (bed), eating (table), reading or thinking (stool/chair), and reading at night (lampstand). She is providing for the man's full pattern of living. The chapter is teaching us a principle of supporting itinerant ministry: substantial hospitality means more than food. Travelers need rest, privacy, study-space, light to read by. The Shunammite's gift is comprehensive. The chapter is also showing us a kind of person — the spiritually perceptive woman of substance who uses her resources to support the LORD's servants. The pattern will recur (Lydia in Acts 16; the women who supported Jesus in Luke 8); the Shunammite is one of its first major exemplars.

And it fell on a day, that he came thither, and he turned into the chamber, and lay there. And he said to Gehazi his servant, Call this Shunammite. And when he had called her, she stood before him. And he said unto him, Say now unto her, Behold, thou hast been careful for us with all this care; what is to be done for thee? wouldest thou be spoken for to the king, or to the captain of the host? And she answered, I dwell among mine own people. Guidance: Elisha's offer and her contented refusal. The day arrives when Elisha rests in the new chamber. From there he calls Gehazi — his servant (the same role Elisha had played for Elijah). 'Call this Shunammite.' The woman comes; she stands before him. Elisha speaks through Gehazi (a formal protocol — prophet to servant to inquirer): 'Behold, thou hast been careful for us with all this care; what is to be done for thee?' Hebrew mah la'asot lakh — 'what is to be done for you?' He wants to repay her hospitality. He proposes two specific possibilities: 'Wouldest thou be spoken for to the king, or to the captain of the host?' Two channels of influence — political (the king) and military (the army commander). Elisha has access to both; he can intercede for her. Many a person in her position would have welcomed such intercession — perhaps for tax relief, perhaps for some judicial matter. Her answer is dignified and surprising: 'I dwell among mine own people.' Hebrew be-tokh ammi anokhi yoshevet — 'among my own people I am dwelling.' She is saying: I have what I need; I am well-settled; I have no need that requires royal intercession. She has not been hospitable to extract favors. Her refusal is not pride but contentment. The chapter is showing us the rare and beautiful posture of generous-without-self-interest. Most generosity has a price-tag — an unspoken expectation of reciprocity. The Shunammite refuses to put a price on her hospitality. She gave because she perceived Elisha's holiness; she did not give to receive. The chapter is teaching us a leadership principle for those served by generous people. Do not assume their service is for hire; some give freely. The Shunammite teaches Elisha that her hospitality cannot be repaid with influence. She wants nothing. The chapter is also setting up the next development. Elisha will not let her go unrewarded; he will ask Gehazi a more probing question to identify what she actually needs but has not voiced.

And he said, What then is to be done for her? And Gehazi answered, Verily she hath no child, and her husband is old. And he said, Call her. And when he had called her, she stood in the door. And he said, About this season, according to the time of life, thou shalt embrace a son. And she said, Nay, my lord, thou man of God, do not lie unto thine handmaid. Guidance: The promise of a son. After her refusal, Elisha presses Gehazi: 'What then is to be done for her?' What is her actual unspoken need? Gehazi's perceptive answer: 'Verily she hath no child, and her husband is old.' The Hebrew avval ben ein-lah ve-ishah zaqen — 'truly she has no son, and her husband is old.' In Israelite culture, a childless wife with an aging husband faced bleak prospects. Without a son, the family line ended; without an heir, all the prosperity she now enjoyed would pass to others at her husband's death. She was contentedly living in the present, but the future was dark. Gehazi has identified the loss she has accepted but never articulated. Elisha calls her again. She stands in the doorway. The oracle: 'About this season, according to the time of life, thou shalt embrace a son.' Hebrew la-moed ha-zeh ka-et chayyah at choveqet ben — 'at this set time, at the time of life, you will be embracing a son.' (The phrase 'time of life' — ka-et chayyah — is the same idiom used in Gen 18:10 for the LORD's promise to Sarah; the parallel is deliberate. The Shunammite is being given a Sarah-style miracle.) Her response is one of the most poignant in the prophetic narratives: 'Nay, my lord, thou man of God, do not lie unto thine handmaid.' Hebrew al-tekhazev be-shifchatekha — 'do not falsify to your handmaid.' She has long since stopped letting herself hope for a child; she has internalized the disappointment. The promise of a son is precisely the hope she had buried. To hear it spoken raises the buried hope; if it is not true, the renewed disappointment will be worse than the original. The chapter is showing us a woman who is honest about her wound. She does not pretend the offer is unwelcome; she does not pretend she does not desire a child. She names her fear precisely: do not lie to me about this. The chapter is teaching us a deep pastoral truth. When a prophet speaks comfort, the people most aware of their pain are the most cautious about receiving it. Hope renewed is exposure; the Shunammite has good reason to be cautious. Elisha does not retract or argue; he lets the oracle stand. The verification will be biological.

And the woman conceived, and bare a son at that season that Elisha had said unto her, according to the time of life. Guidance: The verification. The narrator's single-verse confirmation. 'The woman conceived, and bare a son.' The promise is verified by the most concrete possible test — a birth, at the exact predicted time ('that season... according to the time of life' — the precise window Elisha had specified). The chapter is showing us the LORD's word as biological fact. The prophecy was not metaphor; it was a specific promise that produced a specific child at a specific time. The Shunammite's caution — 'do not lie' — has been answered with the only possible reply: a living son. The chapter is teaching us that the LORD's promises to the obscurely faithful often arrive with the same precision as His promises to patriarchs. Sarah's son came; the Shunammite's son came. The same divine fidelity operates at every scale. The chapter is also preparing the dramatic reversal that follows. The promise has been fulfilled; the joy is presumably immense; the family seems set. The next paragraph will show the child grown and stricken — the same gift seemingly snatched away. The chapter is teaching us that the LORD's gifts sometimes pass through testing-fires. The Shunammite's faith is about to be tested far more than at the first promise.

And when the child was grown, it fell on a day, that he went out to his father to the reapers. And he said unto his father, My head, my head. And he said to a lad, Carry him to his mother. And when he had taken him, and brought him to his mother, he sat on her knees till noon, and then died. Guidance: The catastrophe. Time passes. The promised son grows — old enough to walk to the fields, accompany his father, observe the harvest work. The narrator preserves the ordinary tenderness of the scene. The child, in the heat of the fields, suffers a sudden head-pain crisis. 'My head, my head' — Hebrew roshi roshi — heart-rending in its brevity. The cry could be sunstroke (the most common medical inference — heatstroke can develop suddenly in children in agricultural heat), it could be aneurysm, it could be cerebral hemorrhage. Whatever the specific medical event, it strikes with brutal speed. The father, presumably busy with the reapers, instructs a lad to carry the child to his mother. The mother, who had pleaded 'do not lie to me' about the possibility of a son, now holds him on her knees through the morning into noon. 'He sat on her knees till noon, and then died.' Hebrew vayyeshev al-birkeha ad-ha-tzaharayim vayyamot — 'he sat on her knees until noon, and he died.' The narrative pacing is excruciating. She holds him; she waits; she watches; she sees him slip away. The chapter is showing us the LORD's gift seemingly reclaimed. The Shunammite's deepest fear — that Elisha might be lying about the son — has come true in a darker form. The son was given, then taken; the truth of the promise has become the truth of the loss. The chapter is teaching us that the LORD sometimes lets His gifts pass through death. The promise was not a lie when given; the death does not falsify the promise; but the death produces the suffering that tests whether the gift's recipient still trusts the Giver. The Shunammite will not weep loudly; she will not break; she will do something extraordinary. The narrative is preparing for one of the great faith-responses in the OT.

And she went up, and laid him on the bed of the man of God, and shut the door upon him, and went out. And she called unto her husband, and said, Send me, I pray thee, one of the young men, and one of the asses, that I may run to the man of God, and come again. And he said, Wherefore wilt thou go to him to day? it is neither new moon, nor sabbath. And she said, It shall be well. Guidance: The Shunammite's secret plan. The mother does not lay her dead son in her own bed or in a place of mourning. She takes him to Elisha's chamber — 'the bed of the man of God' — and lays him there. The location is theological: she is connecting the dead child to the prophet whose oracle had brought him. She shuts the door behind her. She does not tell her husband the child is dead; she tells no one. The chapter is showing us extraordinary composure. The Shunammite has decided what she will do, and she will not let news of the death spread through her household or village before she can act. She calls to her husband: 'Send me, I pray thee, one of the young men, and one of the asses, that I may run to the man of God, and come again.' She requests transport — a young attendant and a donkey — to reach Elisha. She does not say her son is dead; she only requests the trip. Her husband, sensing something unusual, asks: 'Wherefore wilt thou go to him to day? it is neither new moon, nor sabbath.' The pious pattern was apparently to visit the prophet on new moons and sabbaths (the religiously significant days). A non-festal weekday visit was out of the ordinary. She would not normally make such a trip. The Shunammite's reply is brief and elliptical: 'It shall be well.' Hebrew shalom — 'peace.' One word, ambiguous: it could mean 'it is well [for me to go]'; or 'all will be well'; or 'goodbye.' She does not lie; she does not explain. She is keeping the news to herself until she can confront Elisha. The chapter is teaching us about a mother's faith. She is grieving — but grief has not driven her to despair. She is going to the prophet who brought the promise, to demand that the promise be honored. The dead son lies on the prophet's bed; the mother is on her way to the prophet. The connection is exactly the one she intends.

Then she saddled an ass, and said to her servant, Drive, and go forward; slack not thy riding for me, except I bid thee. So she went and came unto the man of God to mount Carmel. And it came to pass, when the man of God saw her afar off, that he said to Gehazi his servant, Behold, yonder is that Shunammite. Guidance: The urgent ride to Carmel. The Shunammite saddles the donkey and commands her servant: 'Drive, and go forward; slack not thy riding for me, except I bid thee.' Hebrew nehag va-lekh al-ta'tzar-li lirkov — 'drive and go; do not restrain me from riding.' She wants maximum speed; the servant is not to slow the pace out of concern for her comfort. She is riding to the prophet as fast as a donkey can take her. The destination: 'Mount Carmel.' The same mountain of Elijah's great showdown — Elisha had a residence or regular retreat there. Carmel is roughly 15-20 miles from Shunem; a determined ride could cover the distance in 3-4 hours. Elisha sees her approaching from a distance. 'Behold, yonder is that Shunammite.' The chapter is showing us prophetic perception combined with prophetic ignorance. Elisha can see her and identify her at a distance (perhaps simply visually; perhaps with some additional perceptive gift). But, as the next verses will show, he does not yet know why she is coming. The LORD has not revealed the specific cause; the prophet must inquire to know. The chapter is teaching us that even great prophets do not have automatic omniscience. The LORD reveals specific things at specific times; outside those revelations, the prophet is on the same footing as anyone else for inferring what is happening.

Run now, I pray thee, to meet her, and say unto her, Is it well with thee? is it well with thy husband? is it well with the child? And she answered, It is well. And when she came to the man of God to the hill, she caught him by the feet: but Gehazi came near to thrust her away. And the man of God said, Let her alone; for her soul is vexed within her: and the LORD hath hid it from me, and hath not told me. Guidance: Gehazi's questions, her cryptic answer, and her grasp of the prophet's feet. Elisha dispatches Gehazi to meet her: 'Is it well with thee? is it well with thy husband? is it well with the child?' Three concerned inquiries — Hebrew ha-shalom lakh ha-shalom le-ishekh ha-shalom la-yaled. Same shalom — well/peace — that she had used to her husband earlier. Her answer to Gehazi: 'It is well.' Hebrew shalom — peace. The same word. To Gehazi she will not unfold her grief; she keeps her interview reserved for Elisha himself. The Hebrew is ambiguous: she could be saying 'all is well [I will speak with Elisha]'; or 'peace [I cannot speak about this here]'; or 'shalom [a formal greeting]' as deflection. She reaches Elisha. 'She caught him by the feet.' Hebrew vatachazek be-raglav — 'she seized his feet.' The gesture is intense — a desperate clinging, falling to the ground, embracing the prophet's feet. Gehazi, presumably embarrassed by the seeming impropriety, moves to push her away. Elisha intervenes: 'Let her alone; for her soul is vexed within her.' Hebrew ki-nafshah marah-lah — 'for her soul is bitter to her.' Whatever the exact cause, Elisha perceives that she is in deep distress. He adds a striking confession: 'The LORD hath hid it from me, and hath not told me.' Hebrew YHWH he'lim mimmenni ve-lo higgid li — 'the LORD has hidden from me and has not told me.' Elisha admits he does not know what is wrong. The chapter is showing us the prophet's humility. He could pretend; he could fish for information; he could speak generic comfort while not knowing the specifics. Instead he honestly says: I do not know. The LORD has not yet told me. The chapter is teaching us that authentic prophetic ministry is candid about its limits. Elisha is not omniscient; he depends on what the LORD reveals to him; he does not bluff what he does not know. The Shunammite's clinging is not stopped because of impropriety; it is accepted as the proper register of her distress. Elisha now turns to listen.

Then she said, Did I desire a son of my lord? did I not say, Do not deceive me? Guidance: The Shunammite's reproach. She speaks at last. Two short questions — packed with grief and accusation. 'Did I desire a son of my lord?' Hebrew ha-sha'alti ven me'et adoni — 'did I ask a son from my lord?' She had not asked for a son. The promise had been unsolicited; she had not begged. The gift was given without her request. 'Did I not say, Do not deceive me?' Hebrew ha-lo amarti lo tashleh oti — 'did I not say, do not mislead me?' She had warned Elisha not to lie to her. The promise had been given over her protest. Now the gift has been taken away; what she had feared has happened in the worst possible form. The chapter is showing us a faith that knows how to confront. The Shunammite is not silent. She is not passively accepting. She is also not raging at the LORD; she is raging at the prophet whose oracle she had received with such caution. Her two questions are theologically loaded: if the promise was unsolicited and the gift has been taken away, what was the gift for? Was it a mockery? Was it a test? She demands answers. The chapter is teaching us that authentic faith sometimes confronts the LORD's representative with the unfinished business of the LORD's promise. The Shunammite is not unfaithful for protesting; she is profoundly faithful — she came to Elisha with the protest rather than letting it sit in her heart as quiet bitterness. She names the gap between what was promised and what has happened. The chapter is also showing us that the LORD's people in their pain are allowed to grieve at His prophets. Elisha will not punish her for the confrontation; he will receive it and act on it. The protest is part of how the faithful do business with the LORD: they tell Him when His promises seem to have failed, and they expect Him to respond.

Then he said to Gehazi, Gird up thy loins, and take my staff in thine hand, and go thy way: if thou meet any man, salute him not; and if any salute thee, answer him not again: and lay my staff upon the face of the child. And Gehazi passed on before them, and laid the staff upon the face of the child; but there was neither voice, nor hearing. And when Elisha was come into the house, behold, the child was dead, and laid upon his bed. He went in therefore, and shut the door upon them twain, and prayed unto the LORD. And he went up, and lay upon the child, and put his mouth upon his mouth, and his eyes upon his eyes, and his hands upon his hands: and he stretched himself upon the child; and the flesh of the child waxed warm. Then he returned, and walked in the house to and fro; and went up, and stretched himself upon him: and the child sneezed seven times, and the child opened his eyes. Guidance: The resurrection. Elisha arrives at the Shunammite's house. The child lies on his bed — the prophet's bed where the mother had laid him. The chamber is the same one she had built; the bed is the one she furnished. Elisha enters; he shuts the door behind him; he and the child are alone. The Shunammite, presumably, remains outside; the act of resurrection is a private intercession, not a spectacle. 'He shut the door upon them twain, and prayed unto the LORD.' Hebrew vayyithpallel el-YHWH — 'he prayed to the LORD.' The chapter is showing us prophetic dependence. Elisha does not act on his own initiative; he prays first. The miracle will be the LORD's, not Elisha's. Then the physical action: 'He went up, and lay upon the child, and put his mouth upon his mouth, and his eyes upon his eyes, and his hands upon his hands: and he stretched himself upon the child.' Mouth-to-mouth, eyes-to-eyes, hands-to-hands. The prophet's full body laid over the boy's full body. (Elijah had used a similar action with the Zarephath widow's son in 1 Kgs 17:21.) The act is symbolic of life-transfer: the prophet's living body presses against the dead boy's body. The first stage: 'The flesh of the child waxed warm.' Hebrew vayyacham besar ha-yaled — the boy's flesh became warm. The cold of death begins to retreat. But the boy is not yet awake. Elisha gets up. He walks 'to and fro' in the house — pacing, perhaps continuing to pray, perhaps gathering himself for a second attempt. Then he returns; stretches himself on the boy again. The second stage: 'The child sneezed seven times, and the child opened his eyes.' Seven sneezes — the dramatic resumption of breath, perhaps the boy's lungs clearing. The number seven (the covenantal number) is likely meaningful: the boy is restored fully, completely. He opens his eyes. The chapter is teaching us multiple foundational truths. (1) Resurrection requires more than prophetic word; it requires prophetic presence and prophetic prayer. The staff did nothing; the body-to-body contact and the persistent prayer succeed. (2) The miracle is granted on a process basis, not instantaneously. The flesh warms; the prophet steps away; he returns; the boy sneezes; he opens his eyes. The full restoration takes time and continued intercession. (3) Resurrection is not Elisha's power but the LORD's, channeled through Elisha's intercession. The prophet's body and prayers are the instruments; the life-giving Spirit is the LORD's. The chapter is preparing for the parallel act in 13:21 (the man who came to life touching Elisha's bones) — confirmation that the resurrection power was the LORD's, not the prophet's, and could operate even through the prophet's remains.

And he called Gehazi, and said, Call this Shunammite. So he called her. And when she was come in unto him, he said, Take up thy son. Then she went in, and fell at his feet, and bowed herself to the ground, and took up her son, and went out. Guidance: The reunion. Elisha calls Gehazi and instructs him to bring the Shunammite. She enters. Elisha's three-word command: 'Take up thy son.' Hebrew se'i venekh — 'lift up your son.' That is all. No oration; no theological framing; no extraction of further requests. The boy is restored; she may take him. Her response is exactly proportionate: 'She went in, and fell at his feet, and bowed herself to the ground.' The same posture as her earlier clinging at his feet — but now in worship rather than supplication. The dead son has become the living son again; she falls before the prophet through whom the LORD has done this. 'And took up her son, and went out.' The chapter ends the resurrection narrative with deliberate simplicity. No celebration scene; no dialogue between mother and restored son; no community gathering. The mother takes her son and leaves the chamber. The chapter is teaching us that the most profound moments often require the least adornment. The resurrection narrative does not become a sermon-illustration that overstays; it ends with the mother taking her child and going out. The reader is left with the image of restoration without commentary. The chapter is also showing us the proper response to such miracles. Worship before the LORD's prophet (recognition of the LORD's agent); receive the gift back; carry on with life. The Shunammite does not parade the restored son through the village; she takes him home.

And Elisha came again to Gilgal: and there was a dearth in the land; and the sons of the prophets were sitting before him: and he said unto his servant, Set on the great pot, and seethe pottage for the sons of the prophets. And one went out into the field to gather herbs, and found a wild vine, and gathered thereof wild gourds his lap full, and came and shred them into the pot of pottage: for they knew them not. So they poured out for the men to eat. And it came to pass, as they were eating of the pottage, that they cried out, and said, O thou man of God, there is death in the pot. And they could not eat thereof. And he said, Then bring meal. And he cast it into the pot; and he said, Pour out for the people, that they may eat. And there was no harm in the pot. Guidance: The deadly pot at Gilgal. The chapter shifts to a new setting — Gilgal, the same Gilgal where the chapter-2 farewell tour had begun. There is famine in the land (Hebrew ra'av — likely the early stages of the seven-year famine prophesied in 8:1). The sons of the prophets — the prophetic school members — are gathered around Elisha. He instructs his servant to prepare a large pot of stew (nazid — vegetable stew) for the whole company. One of the students goes foraging for greens. He finds a 'wild vine' (gefen sadeh — vine of the field) bearing 'wild gourds' (paqu'ot sadeh — possibly the colocynth, Citrullus colocynthis, a desert-vine fruit; or possibly a similar bitter-melon family plant). He fills his lap with them and brings them home; he shreds them into the pot. The Hebrew specifies: 'they knew them not.' The students do not recognize what the foraged plant is. When the stew is poured for the meal, they begin to taste it — and immediately recognize bitterness or toxicity. 'O thou man of God, there is death in the pot.' Hebrew mavet ba-sir ish ha-elohim — 'death in the pot, man of God.' (Colocynth is famously bitter and acts as a violent purgative; sufficient quantity could be life-threatening.) They cannot eat it. Elisha's remedy: 'Then bring meal.' Hebrew qachu qemach — bring flour. He casts the flour into the pot. The toxicity is neutralized: 'There was no harm in the pot.' The chapter is teaching us several things. (1) The LORD's prophet is the safety-net for the prophetic community. When the students nearly poisoned themselves, Elisha's intervention saved them. (2) The remedy is symbolic: the contamination of death is removed not by removing the bad ingredient but by adding something else. The flour is not literally a chemical antidote to colocynth; the addition is symbolic of the LORD's transformative power working through ordinary means. (3) Famine-time foraging is risky; community members may innocently introduce dangers. The remedy is leadership intervention, not blame. The chapter is also showing us the everyday character of Elisha's miracles. After a resurrection, the very next scene is a kitchen rescue. The prophet's ministry reaches from raising the dead to detoxifying stew. Both ends serve the LORD's people in their actual situations.

He instructs his servant to prepare a large pot of stew (nazid — vegetable stew) for the whole company. One of the students goes foraging for greens. He finds a 'wild vine' (gefen sadeh — vine of the field) bearing 'wild gourds' (paqu'ot sadeh — possibly the colocynth, Citrullus colocynthis, a desert-vine fruit; or possibly a similar bitter-melon family plant). He fills his lap with them and brings them home; he shreds them into the pot. The Hebrew specifies: 'they knew them not.' The students do not recognize what the foraged plant is. When the stew is poured for the meal, they begin to taste it — and immediately recognize bitterness or toxicity. 'O thou man of God, there is death in the pot.' Hebrew mavet ba-sir ish ha-elohim — 'death in the pot, man of God.' (Colocynth is famously bitter and acts as a violent purgative; sufficient quantity could be life-threatening.) They cannot eat it. Elisha's remedy: 'Then bring meal.' Hebrew qachu qemach — bring flour. He casts the flour into the pot. The toxicity is neutralized: 'There was no harm in the pot.' The chapter is teaching us several things. (1) The LORD's prophet is the safety-net for the prophetic community. When the students nearly poisoned themselves, Elisha's intervention saved them. (2) The remedy is symbolic: the contamination of death is removed not by removing the bad ingredient but by adding something else. The flour is not literally a chemical antidote to colocynth; the addition is symbolic of the LORD's transformative power working through ordinary means. (3) Famine-time foraging is risky; community members may innocently introduce dangers. The remedy is leadership intervention, not blame. The chapter is also showing us the everyday character of Elisha's miracles. After a resurrection, the very next scene is a kitchen rescue. The prophet's ministry reaches from raising the dead to detoxifying stew. Both ends serve the LORD's people in their actual situations.

And Elisha came again to Gilgal: and there was a dearth in the land; and the sons of the prophets were sitting before him: and he said unto his servant, Set on the great pot, and seethe pottage for the sons of the prophets. And one went out into the field to gather herbs, and found a wild vine, and gathered thereof wild gourds his lap full, and came and shred them into the pot of pottage: for they knew them not. So they poured out for the men to eat. And it came to pass, as they were eating of the pottage, that they cried out, and said, O thou man of God, there is death in the pot. And they could not eat thereof. And he said, Then bring meal. And he cast it into the pot; and he said, Pour out for the people, that they may eat. And there was no harm in the pot. And there came a man from Baalshalisha, and brought the man of God bread of the firstfruits, twenty loaves of barley, and full ears of corn in the husk thereof. And he said, Give unto the people, that they may eat. And his servitor said, What, should I set this before an hundred men? He said again, Give the people, that they may eat: for thus saith the LORD, They shall eat, and shall leave thereof. So he set it before them, and they did eat, and left thereof, according to the word of the LORD. Guidance: The twenty barley loaves and the hundred. A man from Baal-shalisha — a town in the central highlands, the exact location debated — brings Elisha the firstfruits gift: 'twenty loaves of barley, and full ears of corn in the husk thereof.' Twenty small barley-loaves and some fresh ears of grain. The offering is the firstfruits (bikkurim) — the agricultural tithe Israelites brought to the LORD's representatives. The chapter is showing us that even in famine, faithful Israelites continued to bring firstfruits — a remarkable detail of covenantal faithfulness in scarcity. Elisha instructs his servant: 'Give unto the people, that they may eat.' The bread is to be distributed to the prophetic community. The servant's objection is mathematically reasonable: 'What, should I set this before an hundred men?' Twenty small loaves cannot feed 100 men. Elisha repeats the command with the LORD's promise: 'Give the people, that they may eat: for thus saith the LORD, They shall eat, and shall leave thereof.' The multiplication will happen as the bread is distributed. The result: 'They did eat, and left thereof, according to the word of the LORD.' Hebrew vayyochelu va-yyotiru ki-devar YHWH — 'they ate and left over, according to the word of the LORD.' (The narrative pattern is the obvious antecedent of Jesus' feeding of 5,000 and 4,000 in the Gospels. Mark 6 and Mark 8 deliberately echo this story. Jesus' provision of bread for the multitude is presented as the new-covenant fulfillment of the Elisha-bread sign.) The chapter is teaching us several principles. (1) The LORD multiplies what is given to Him. The faithful man brought firstfruits in famine; the LORD made the firstfruits feed a community. The act of giving in scarcity became the basis of provision. (2) Servants of the LORD must sometimes obey commands that seem arithmetically impossible. The servant's objection was numerically sensible; the prophet's command was supernaturally informed. Both were right within their domains; the servant's obedience let the supernatural overflow into the natural. (3) The LORD's provision usually exceeds bare need. The bread did not just feed the 100 with mathematical exactness; it 'left thereof' — there was surplus. The LORD's economy includes excess that testifies to His abundance. The chapter is concluding the four-fold miracle sequence with this multiplication-image: oil that filled all vessels, son who lived after dying, pot that became safe, bread that fed beyond its capacity. Together, these four miracles establish Elisha's ministry as one of providence-restoration. Whatever the LORD's covenant people lack — funds, family, food-safety, abundance — the prophet stands ready to supply through divinely-given resources. The chapter is teaching us the comprehensive scope of the LORD's care: nothing is too small (a widow's oil) and nothing is too large (a dead child); nothing is too dangerous (a poisoned pot) and nothing is too miraculous (multiplication of bread). The same LORD addresses every dimension.
